basics.description: **Note:** Please refer to the following before installing the solution: • Review the solution [Release Notes](https://github.com/Azure/Azure-Sentinel/tree/master/Solutions/UniFi%20Syslog%20%28CCF%29/ReleaseNotes.md) • There may be [known issues](https://aka.ms/sentinelsolutionsknownissues) pertaining to this Solution, please refer to them before installing. The [UniFi](https://www.ui.com/) Syslog solution for Microsoft Sentinel ingests UDM, switch and access-point syslog telemetry using the Codeless Connector Framework (CCF) Push pattern. A self-hosted Logstash relay parses the UniFi dual-timestamp format, normalises CEF and synthetic-CEF events, enriches with MaxMind GeoIP/ASN and FireHOL/Spamhaus/Tor threat-intel feeds at ingest time, and POSTs to the Data Collection Endpoint provisioned by this connector. **Pipeline split:** - CEF + synthetic-CEF security events land in **CommonSecurityLog** (firewall zones, sudo, dnsmasq-dhcp, AP kernel WiFi, ubios gateway signals, switch link state, sshd). - Forensic / non-CEF events land in **Unifi_Syslog_CL** as a structured catch-all. **Ships:** 8 analytic rules (TI match, IDS/IPS, SSH brute force, firewall drops, admin-from-WAN, VPN brute force, Tor traffic, critical-severity) and 10 hunting queries (top attackers, port scanners, app-port probes, first-seen sources, geo anomaly, sudo audit, SSH targets, WiFi deauth flood, DHCP anomalies, TI feed effectiveness). **Pre-requisites:** 1. A Linux host reachable from your UniFi devices on UDP/TCP 514 (UnRAID, Docker host, k8s, VM). 2. The Logstash container shipped with this Solution under its `Logstash/` subfolder (Dockerfile + logstash.conf + MaxMind/TI refreshers). 3. (Optional but recommended) A MaxMind account for GeoIP/ASN enrichment - free GeoLite2 tier is sufficient. **Data Connectors:** 1, **Workbooks:** 1, **Analytic Rules:** 8, **Hunting Queries:** 10 [Learn more about Microsoft Sentinel](https://aka.ms/azuresentinel) | [Learn more about Solutions](https://aka.ms/azuresentinelsolutionsdoc)

options.text: This Solution installs the data connector for UniFi Syslog (CCF Push) (Preview). You can get UniFi Syslog (CCF Push) (Preview) data in your Microsoft Sentinel workspace. After installing the solution, configure and enable this data connector by following guidance in Manage solution view.

link.label: Learn more about connecting data sources

options.text: This solution installs workbook(s) to help you gain insights into the telemetry collected in Microsoft Sentinel. After installing the solution, start using the workbook in Manage solution view.

The UniFi Syslog workbook delivers end-to-end forensic and security visibility over UDM / switch / AP syslog data. Eight tabs cover incidents, investigation, hunts, identity, world-map geo, firewall analysis, pipeline health, and a hero KPI overview - powered by the 8 analytic rules, 10 hunting queries, MaxMind GeoIP/ASN enrichment, and FireHOL/Spamhaus/Tor TI feeds shipped with the connector.
This solution installs the following analytic rule templates. After installing the solution, create and enable analytic rules in Manage solution view.

elements.name: analytic1
elements.label: UniFi: Threat-intel match on inbound traffic

options.text: Identifies inbound traffic whose source IP matched a known-bad TI feed (FireHOL Level 1, Spamhaus DROP/EDROP, or Tor exit). Severity reflects the highest-confidence feed that matched. Tune by changing the ThreatSeverity threshold.

Identifies UniFi Network IDS (Suricata + CINS / Emerging Threats rulesets) detections. UniFi marks these severity 3 natively - this rule promotes them to Medium for incident handling. Also covers Suricata events that leak via plain syslog.
Identifies 5+ SSH authentication failures from a single source IP within 15 minutes against UniFi consoles. Severity elevated to Critical when the source IP also matches a TI feed.

elements.name: analytic4
elements.label: UniFi: Repeated firewall drops from single source

options.text: Identifies 50+ firewall drop or reject events from the same source IP within an hour - likely a port scan, brute force probe, or misconfigured client. Uses UniFi zone-based firewall events parsed into CEF (DeviceEventClassID like LAN_LAN / WAN_LOCAL).

elements.name: analytic5
elements.label: UniFi: Admin activity from non-LAN source

options.text: Identifies successful SSH logins or admin CEF events originating from a source IP outside RFC1918 private ranges. These should be rare in a healthy network (VPN traffic should appear as private); investigate every match to confirm it was an authorised remote login.

elements.name: analytic6
elements.label: UniFi: VPN authentication failures (possible brute force)

options.text: Identifies 10+ VPN authentication failures from a single source IP within one hour. Covers both CEF events in CommonSecurityLog and plain-syslog VPN failures from openvpn, wireguard, ipsec, strongswan, charon and xl2tpd daemons in Unifi_Syslog_CL.

elements.name: analytic7
elements.label: UniFi: Traffic from Tor exit node

options.text: Identifies inbound traffic originating from a Tor exit node (identified at ingest time via the Tor exit-node feed). Legitimate use cases exist, but Tor is also a common cover for reconnaissance and credential stuffing.

Identifies critical-severity events emitted by UniFi devices - power loss, fan failure, hardware fault, or security crisis. Covers CEF events (LogSeverity 8+) in CommonSecurityLog and plain-syslog events with SyslogSeverity emergency/alert/critical.
This solution installs the following hunting queries. After installing the solution, run these hunting queries to hunt for threats in Manage solution view.

Ranks TI-flagged source IPs by activity volume in the last 24h. Use this to prioritise blocklist additions or investigate persistent actors that hit your perimeter. This hunting query depends on UnifiSyslogCCF data connector (CommonSecurityLog Parser or Table)

Lists external sources hitting 10 or more distinct destination ports within a 5-minute window - the classic port-scan signature. Filters to non-RFC1918 sources only to suppress noise from internal LAN scanners. This hunting query depends on UnifiSyslogCCF data connector (CommonSecurityLog Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery3
elements.label: UniFi: TI-flagged probes against application ports

options.text: Aggregates known-bad sources by application port being targeted (SSH, RDP, SMB, MSSQL, HTTP/S, Postgres, VNC, Plex, etc.). Reveals which of your services are getting the most scanner attention. This hunting query depends on UnifiSyslogCCF data connector (CommonSecurityLog Parser or Table)

Lists external IPs observed in the last hour that have never appeared in the preceding 30 days. New-source anomalies often correlate with attack campaigns and reconnaissance waves. This hunting query depends on UnifiSyslogCCF data connector (CommonSecurityLog Parser or Table)

Lists admin or SSH activity originating from countries outside your normal operating geography. Edit the expected_countries list at the top of the query to match your environment. This hunting query depends on UnifiSyslogCCF data connector (CommonSecurityLog Parser or Table)

Lists sudo events not from known service accounts (unifi-protect, unifi, ubios-udapi). Real admin sudo activity to root warrants attention. Tune the system_users list at the top of the query for your environment. This hunting query depends on UnifiSyslogCCF data connector (CommonSecurityLog Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery7
elements.label: UniFi: SSH brute force - most-targeted accounts

options.text: Aggregates SSH authentication failures by the username being targeted. Reveals what attackers think your accounts are named - useful for tightening account lockdown and removing default usernames. This hunting query depends on UnifiSyslogCCF data connector (CommonSecurityLog Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery8
elements.label: UniFi: WiFi deauth flooding (possible Karma / Evil Twin)

options.text: Lists APs that observed 20 or more WiFi deauth or disassoc events within a 5-minute window. Could be normal roaming chaos, or active deauth attack (Karma, Evil Twin, WPA handshake capture). This hunting query depends on UnifiSyslogCCF data connector (CommonSecurityLog Parser or Table)

Lists DHCP NAK (server denied) and DECLINE (client refused offer, usually IP conflict) bursts. Volume spikes can mean rogue DHCP, address-pool exhaustion, or a misconfigured client. This hunting query depends on UnifiSyslogCCF data connector (CommonSecurityLog Parser or Table)

Lists how many hits each threat-intel feed produced over the last 7 days. Useful for deciding whether a feed is pulling its weight or needs replacing in the Logstash relay configuration. This hunting query depends on UnifiSyslogCCF data connector (CommonSecurityLog Parser or Table)
